pattern: A row can only ever contribute its own largest few values, so shrink each row to its best limits[i] candidates first, then pick the overall best k from that combined pool.
hints: Within a single row, if you were only allowed elements from that row, you would obviously take its largest ones first.
That means any value outside a row's top limits[i] entries can never belong to an optimal selection -- discard the rest before combining rows.
Merge every row's surviving candidates into one list, sort it, and sum the largest k of them.

edge_inputs.description: single column, every row allows one pick
k is zero, sum must be zero regardless of grid
a row limit of zero excludes that row entirely
one row has the largest single value but a tiny limit, so the top overall values still come from elsewhere
all zeros, sum stays zero even with a full budget
k equal to the full sum of all limits, taking everything each row allows

stress.description: moderately sized grid with random per-row limits and a random overall budget
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    dim = max(1, min(60, size // 6 or 1))
    n = dim
    m = dim
    grid = [[rng.randint(0, 100000) for _ in range(m)] for _ in range(n)]
    limits = [rng.randint(0, m) for _ in range(n)]
    total = sum(limits)
    k = rng.randint(0, min(n * m, total)) if total > 0 else 0
    return [grid, limits, k]
